title: Protect PCH
text:
In honor of Kellie Standish, Colby Tucker, and everyone impacted by preventable tragedies on Ventura County’s Pacific Coast Highway, we’re calling on Ventura County and Caltrans to take immediate action to make PCH safer.
Real change happens when our community speaks with one voice. The more our community takes action, the harder we are to ignore.


Here are four impactful ways you can join us and make your voice heard.


  
  
    1
    
      Send the Letter to Your Representatives
      A letter has already been drafted for you. It is addressed to five decision-makers who have the power to connect and collaborate with Caltrans, fund safety improvements, and demand action on PCH.

      How to send it:
      Click Email a Decision Maker to send the letter directly. Email is the fastest and most trackable method. Each representative has a contact form on their official website.

      Who the letter goes to:
      
        
          Matt LaVere — Ventura County Supervisor, District 1
          800 S. Victoria Ave., Ventura
          matt.lavere@venturacounty.gov  ·  brian.brennan@venturacounty.gov
        
        
          Steve Bennett — CA State Assemblymember, District 38
          300 E. Esplanade Dr., Ste. 1790, Oxnard
          assemblymember.bennet@assembly.ca.gov  ·  atticus.reyes@asm.ca.gov  ·  patricia.quiroz@asm.ca.gov
        
        
          Salud Carbajal — U.S. Representative, CA-24
          erica.reyes@mail.house.gov  ·  amisha.deyoung@mail.house.gov
        
        
          Monique Limón — CA State Senator, SD-21
          lupe.lopezgarcia@sen.ca.gov  ·  benjamin.peterson@sen.ca.gov
        
        
          Marlon Regisford — Caltrans District 7, Deputy Director
          marlon.regisford@dot.ca.gov  ·  ryan.w.johnson@dot.ca.gov
        
      

      Mail letters to:
      
        
          California Highway Patrol
          (805) 662-2640
        
        
          Sheriff's Administration — Office of the Sheriff
          800 South Victoria Avenue, Ventura, CA 93009
          (805) 654-2380
        
      
    
  

  
  
    2
    
      Show Up at the Ventura County Board of Supervisors Meetings
      This is the most powerful thing you can do in person. The Ventura County Board of Supervisors oversees county road policy, funds infrastructure, and has direct relationships with Caltrans. Showing up with a crowd sends an unmistakable message.
      View the full schedule of upcoming Ventura County Board of Supervisor meetings here.

      Septmeber 1, 2026:
      
        Date: Tuesday, September 1, 2026
        Time: 8:30 am — arrive by 8:00 am to fill out a speaker card
        Location: Board of Supervisors Hearing Room, Hall of Administration, 800 S. Victoria Ave., Ventura, CA 93009
      

      How to Sign Up to Speak (Pre-Registration — Preferred):
      
        Visit venturacounty.gov/board-of-supervisors and look for the Public Comment registration link for the September 1 meeting.
        Register by 3:30 pm on Sunday, August 31. This ensures you are placed on the speakers list.
        You will receive Zoom call-in information by 6:00 pm if you prefer to comment remotely by phone or video.
      

      How to Submit a Written Comment (if you cannot attend):
      Email the Clerk of the Board at clerkoftheboard@venturacounty.gov by 3:30 pm on Sunday, August 31.
      Subject line: PCH Safety — Public Comment, September 1 Board Meeting
      Your written comment will be entered into the official record and distributed to all five supervisors.

      What to Say (approximately 2–3 minutes):
      You do not need to read from a script. Speak from your experience. Here is a simple structure:
      
        Start: Say your name and what city you are from.
        State the crisis: Two people were killed on PCH near Solimar Beach on June 11 by a drunk driver going 50+ mph who veered into the bike lane. This is at least the ninth fatality on this stretch in recent years.
        Name the problem: PCH has bike lanes and beach access points that invite cyclists — but it operates as a high-speed highway with no physical separation. That gap is killing people.
        Make one ask: Ask the Board to pass a resolution directing Caltrans to conduct an emergency safety review of the Ventura County PCH corridor — or to reduce the speed limit — or to fund physical separation barriers.
        Close personally: Share why this matters to you. I ride this road. My kids access the beach here. A friend of mine was almost hit here.
      

      📋 Tip: Coordinate with other speakers so you cover different angles — personal story, data and infrastructure, family safety, economic impact, equity. Variety is more powerful than repetition.
    
  

  
  
    3
    
      Spread the Word
      
        Forward this page to cyclists, paddlers, hikers, parents, neighbors — anyone who recreates in Ventura.
        Share on local Facebook groups, Nextdoor, cycling club listservs, and neighborhood apps.
        Tag @VenturaCounty and your local representatives when posting about the campaign.
        Contact local media: Ventura County Star, VC Reporter, KCLU (805-986-5258). The crash on June 11 is recent news — reporters are likely still following this story.
      
    
  

  
  
    4
    
      Report Bike Crash Data
      Bikemaps.org is a free citizen science platform where anyone can log a bike crash or near-miss directly from their phone or computer. Adding your experience takes about two minutes.
      Why this matters: official crash data only exists when law enforcement responds and files a report. The vast majority of incidents on PCH go completely unrecorded, making it easy for agencies to claim that a dangerous road is not a priority. Bikemaps.org fills that gap.
      Near-miss data is especially powerful. It captures the danger cyclists experience every day before a fatality occurs, giving decision-makers evidence to act on without waiting for the next death. Every entry — crash, close call, or unsafe condition — contributes to a public dataset that advocates, researchers, and transportation agencies can use to demand improvements.
      We don't have to wait for a fatality to occur to make a case for change.
      Log a crash or near-miss on bikemaps.org →

title: Email a Decision Maker
text:
Personal messages from community members matter. Use the form below to representatives and call for immediate action to protect cyclists, pedestrians, and everyone who travels this corridor. You can use the provided template or add your own story, connection to PCH, and why safer infrastructure matters to you.
When you click Send Email, your default email app will open with the message ready to send.


  
    Who receives this email?
    This message goes to a local city decision maker working on transportation and street safety in Ventura.
    Your voice helps prioritize safer infrastructure for cyclists and pedestrians.
  

  
    
      Your Name *
      
      Please enter your name before sending.
    
    
      Your Neighborhood (optional)
      
    
  

  
    Subject Line
    
  

  
    Your Message
    Honorable Representative,
    
We are writing to you with deep urgency and grief on behalf of our community — residents, cyclists, recreational users, and workers who live and travel throughout Ventura County and the greater tri-county area.

On the evening of June 11, 2026, two people were killed and a third seriously injured on Pacific Coast Highway (State Route 1) near Solimar Beach Road, just north of Ventura. According to the California Highway Patrol and the Ventura County District Attorney's Office, a 24-year-old Oxnard man was traveling at 50–55 mph when he veered his pickup truck into the designated bicycle lane and struck three cyclists from behind. Kellie Standish, 33, of Bend, Oregon, died at the scene. William Colby Tucker, 39, of Ventura, died at Ventura County Medical Center. A third cyclist was hospitalized with serious injuries. The driver was arrested at the scene and has since been charged with two counts of second-degree murder, DUI causing injury, and driving on a suspended license. He is being held without bail.

We write to you today about a separate and independent matter, one that predates June 11 and will outlast it: the documented, chronic failure of the PCH corridor in Ventura County to protect the lives of cyclists and pedestrians.

This is not a problem that began with one driver's choice. According to crash data documented in the California SWITRS database, maintained by the California Highway Patrol and mapped by UC Berkeley's SafeTREC through the TIMS platform, this stretch of State Route 1 has seen at least 11 fatalities and countless serious injuries over recent years across a range of crash types and contributing factors, not only impairment. The infrastructure of this corridor does not require a drunk driver to kill people. It has done so without one. That is the independent public safety crisis we are asking you to address.

This stretch of highway operates at a posted speed limit of 45 mph, with vehicles routinely exceeding that speed. It carries painted bike lanes with no physical separation from high-speed vehicle traffic. It provides beach access points, campgrounds, and recreational entry used by millions of visitors annually across the tri-county region. The infrastructure signals to cyclists, pedestrians, and families that this is a corridor where they are welcome. That signal is not backed by a design that protects their lives.

Painted lines are not protection. They are paint.
We are calling on you to act on the independent, systemic infrastructure failures of this corridor. Specifically, we respectfully urge you to:
- Immediately convene a multi-agency safety summit involving Caltrans District 7, CHP, Ventura County, and local municipalities to assess the full scope of fatalities and serious injuries on PCH between the Los Angeles County line and the City of Ventura — across all crash types and contributing factors.

- Direct Caltrans to conduct an expedited safety study of the Ventura County PCH corridor, separate from and in addition to the ongoing Malibu-focused PCH Master Plan, with specific focus on the stretch near Solimar Beach, Faria Beach, Rincon, and Emma Wood State Beach.

- Pursue emergency protective infrastructure measures in the near term, including physical separation barriers between bicycle lanes and vehicle travel lanes, reduced speed limits through known recreational access zones, enhanced signage, and increased DUI and speed enforcement.

- Explore long-term solutions including dedicated separated bicycle paths, physical medians, and road design that honestly reflects the multi-modal nature of this corridor.

- Secure state and federal funding — including ATP, HSIP, and CMAQ program resources — for safety improvements on this stretch of PCH, which serves both urban and unincorporated communities across county lines.

- Engage directly with affected community members, cyclists, and active transportation advocates in Ventura County in designing any proposed safety solutions.

We acknowledge that improvements to a state highway require coordination across multiple levels of government, and we recognize the complexity of that process. But the families of Kellie Standish and William Colby Tucker, and the families of the others killed on this corridor before them, deserve more than condolences. They deserve to know that their loved ones' deaths will prompt lasting, structural change — independent of the outcome of any criminal proceeding.

We are unequivocal: the driver who chose to drive while impaired and who had previously been warned of the deadly consequences of doing so following a December 2025 DUI arrest bears sole criminal responsibility for the deaths of Kellie and Colby. Nothing in this letter should be read as diminishing that responsibility or as commentary on the pending criminal proceedings. Justice for Kellie and Colby must be pursued fully and without compromise.

We are community members who ride, run, walk, live, and work along the Central Coast. We are not asking for the road to be closed to cars. We are asking you to treat this corridor as the public safety emergency the data shows it to be and to act accordingly.
Thank you for your attention to this critical matter. We welcome the opportunity to meet with your office and with Caltrans to discuss solutions. We stand ready to engage.

Sincerely,
[Your Name]
    0 characters
  

  ✉ Send Email to Decision Maker
  
    Clicking "Send Email" opens your default email application with this message pre-filled —
    you can review it before sending. Nothing is sent automatically.

title: Crash Map
text:
Each dot represents a recorded crash involving a cyclist or pedestrian in Ventura County. Data is sourced from SWITRS using CHP reports and covers 1/1/2020 – 3/31/2026. Click any dot for date, location, and severity details.

title: Safer Streets for Pedestrians & Cyclists
text:
Get Involved
Ready to help make Ventura safer? Fill out our community interest form to let us know how you’d like to contribute — from advocacy to outreach to data collection.
Want to speak up directly? Email a city decision maker →

title: Stay Connected
text:
We’re building a network of Ventura residents, advocates, cyclists, and pedestrians who want to make our streets safer. Whether you want to attend meetings or just stay informed, fill out the form below and we’ll be in touch.

  
    Loading form…
  



  Having trouble with the form?
  Email us directly.
